dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for a cardiac arrest call at an apartment on Jackson Street.
Hello, I'm Lieutenant Morgan, an EMT with this rescue squad, and I just spoke with the fire crew inside. What brought you all out here tonight?
We came back to the apartment-found him lying on the floor in the living room. Doesn't look good.
What did you see before calling 911? Was he breathing or conscious at all?
We don't know. When we found him, he wasn't breathing and had no pulse.
Did you try to stimulate him? Any reaction at all to voice or touch?
We checked his pupils and called 911, but nothing happened after that. He didn't respond at all.
Did the pupils appear large and fixed? Were they equal in size?
Yep-both were large and flat, no reaction to light or stimuli of any kind.
Could you move any limbs or get a reaction from him at all after touching his shoulders or legs?
There was none. He was completely still and unresponsive-no sign of movement at all.
We'll start CPR. Partner, you'll be on BVM while I confirm airway patency now. Get ready.
Airway is still cleared after intubation-King airway is in and condensation is ongoing following each breath. Solid seal.
Breathing is not spontaneous-ongoing BVM is the main support here. What's the respiratory rate and saturation at this time?
Respiration rate is 16 per minute, still irregular. Satellite shows 72%-oxygenation is poor despite BVM. No cyanosis seen at this point.
Bilateral breath sounds are clear-no wheeze, rales, or stridor.
Now let's check for circulation. Partner, take the distal pulse and look at skin color and perfusion.
Pulse is 133 by palpation. Very weak but there. Also confirmed via monitor-same numeric that shows sinus tach on EKG.
Blood pressure is 41/31 by manual auscultation-very low.
Blood glucose is 274-elevated. Per ePCR, no evidence of hypoglycemia on scene or before medication was given.
IV is established in the right bicep. Normal saline running KVO-open and secured. Peripheral pulses are weak but detectable.
What can you tell us about what happened before he went unresponsive and started having cardiac issues?
We came back to the apartment after being out. Found him unresponsive. Still laying there with no signs of breathing or pulse for roughly 20 minutes before we called 911.
He known for drug use-any known medical history or conditions on him besides that, as reported by friends on scene?
Nothing else. Main concern reported is heroin use according to friends before transport began. No documented disease other than that to our knowledge.
Anything known on what he last ate or drank? Friends mentioned he had been out earlier-any more details on that?
He had been drinking earlier-no mention of food, but environs suggest alcohol. Records show he was conscious and mobile before, per witnesses on scene as noted per ePCR.
Did he express any pain or discomfort prior to going unresponsive-and is there any sign of that from you after arrival at the scene today?
No-none at all. He didn't move or react when we sat near him or touched him. No signs of distress or movement reported by myself or anyone else here at the scene initially.
Skin shows no signs of rashes, scars, or burns-appearance is normal. Per ePCR, no bite wounds noted during examination by ALS or BLS crews.
Head and neck have no visible signs of trauma. Pupils are non-reactive per ePCR and verified post-arrival by fire crews prior to ALS.
Oral airway is patent. No visible obstruction, no foreign body detected. Swelling or aspiration risks not found post-intubation or during airway checks on scene.
Post-intubation chest sounds are bilateral and equal. Austic feedback via bag compressions is stable. Per ePCR, no signs of hemothorax or tension pneumothorax noted.
EKG is recording sinus tach at HR of 133. Per ePCR, there were also noted pauses and a brief rhythm change to SVT during resuscitation.
Administered 1 mg epinephrine 1:10,000 IVP at 22:46-per protocol through first dose.
Following epinephrine, given 2 mg naloxone IV immediately post- as part of overdose protocol.
King airway was placed-verified by team nod and confirmation of equal bilateral breath sounds and condensation during bag compressions by both myself and fire crew.
Patient declining post second dose. Rhythm changed to asystole-protocol until further orders adjusted.
ETCO2 levels observed between 40 to 60-documented by ePCR. Consistent with ongoing manual chest compressions.
Rechecked vitals per protocol. BP remains low-41/31. Heart rate still at 133. EKG confirmed sinus tach rhythm as noted via continuous monitoring at 2302.
AutoPulse CPR initiated-machine rhythm on pause converted briefly to SVT. No palpable pulse detected by two providers at time of change-per ePCR.
Second dose of 1 mg epinephrine 1:10,000 given at 22:50, followed by 50 mEq sodium bicarbonate IV at 22:54.
Attempting to restore stability: eCG conclusions trending with changes. Manual CPR resumed as AutoPulse was removed during transport setup per ePCR and supervisor's order.
No pulse confirmed post-AutoPulse removal during transport preparation. BP still reads at 41/31. EKG continuing to show sinus tach.
Third round of 1 mg epinephrine 1:10,000 given at 23:08-again per ePCR and protocol. No return of measureable pulse identifiable.
Transporting this patient to [COMPANY_NAME]-closest advanced cardiac care facility. Initiating ambulance-to-hospital emergency transfer now.
All restraints applied securely across all body regions. Transport safe per protocol.
Fourth dose of 1 mg epinephrine 1:10,000 IVP given upon patient arrival at hospital at 2337.
Pre-hospital tide monitors and ecg strip reviewed-secured and handed over for documentation upon hospital arrival. Review all notes for accurate transition with receiving team.
This patient has been stabilized and prepped per general cardiac arrest protocol. Prehospital intervention complete-follow up needed by hospital staff only now.
GCS per ePCR at 3-Eyes 1, Verbal 1, Motor 1. No change in neuro status based on repeated checks per ePCR.
Initial call: cardiac arrest. Vitals confirmed before first treatment: respiratory rate 16, SpO2 at 72%, BP 41/31, HR 133. All metrics documented via manual charting and EMS equipment per ePCR.
Four doses of epinephrine 1:10,000 via IV confirmed administered per protocol-naloxone also given early for suspected overdose.
Allergies are unknown, and no medical history could be confirmed-patient is 30 years old with reported heroin use as primary risk as noted by friends during the call.
All prehospital treatments match current protocol. An ePCR has been completed and handed to hospital staff for further review and continued care.
No known pain reported-per ePCR, pain score is 0. All observations align with unresponsive patient received on stretcher at destination.
